speaker: interviewer
text: May 9, 1995, interview with Eva Bergmann. Was your father able to transmit the message he got from Professor [? Haltermann? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was. He came to Princeton, and he conveyed the message the way Haltermann had told him. And later on, he told us that all the professors who sat with him, they made a meeting. They sat with him. They were highly interested in what Dad had to convey. And they took the message with-- very seriously. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, in many later years, probably this was not as important anymore, but at the moment, it was of great importance that he conveyed the message the way [? Haltermann ?] had told him. And Daddy was working for a while, still in Princeton with Professor Einstein, and at the university there, which was a delight. Princeton is a beautiful town, and he enjoyed working there. And I worked there also, with a-- I took care of a little boy. He was I think, three years old, and took care of the boy and his family in-- in the household, which was delightful. And later on, my father, of course, was teaching at the New School for Social Research, and here in New York, and also after that, in the City College. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In City College, I'm not quite sure how long he was there, but he was asked to-- to be a professor at the NYU. At the NYU downtown, first, where he traveled every day downtown. We had an apartment in the Bronx, so Dad had to travel all the way downtown. But he had a great amount of students, and taught theoretical physics there, and was very, very happy there. And he also taught for a while in the Bronx at the NYU, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only when he was 75 years old, my father was retiring. Because they prolonged his work, year after year, because the students all liked his teaching. And he was physically able-- also, he was a delicate man, but he was physically able to do that, and enjoyed doing it. My mother actually did not work. She didn't work. And I was working in nursery schools. I worked in three different nursery schools, with pre-kindergarten, and later kindergarten. We also were, for one year, in Schenectady, where my father had taught at Union College for a year, and I worked there also in a nursery school. I liked to be in a nursery school, and I like children, always. And I met Fred in 1951, on a party. And we went together for two years before we married. We didn't marry, only after two years. And we-- I don't know. And, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: You got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I got married, and had our Peter in 1953. He was born 1953. And we-- then I didn't work anymore. Once I had Peter, I did not-- I stopped working in the nursery school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to say something else. I don't remember. Please take your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fred, of course, had belonged to a-- to the synagogue already, before I joined, going with him to synagogue. I went with him to the synagogue on high holidays only. But, as I never had learned Hebrew, I felt quite-- not quite at ease. But I went with him, and enjoyed the beautiful music, and I loved the whole atmosphere of the synagogue. And later on, I always said to Fred, I do want to learn Hebrew. Only two years ago, I made my Bat Mitzvah in our synagogue. And I learned Hebrew-- not fluently, by no means-- only in one year. I-- not even 3/4 of a year. I studied Hebrew, and I loved. And we go both every Friday, and almost every Saturday to the synagogue, which is a German-Jewish synagogue. It's downtown near Lincoln Center, and there are many, many, immigrants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Well, it seems that you have adjusted very well, and we are very glad that you are here to tell us about it, about your background and what you and your family went through to be able to be free at last. And 50 years after the war is over, and thinking back, do you have any thoughts that you would like to leave with Peter, your son, your grandchildren, and for that matter, to the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You see, I'm more than grateful that my parents and I were able to get over here-- and my brother, too, was able to escape the horrors of the Nazis. I am very happy to say that our son, who lived many years with us, was very interested in our past. And I feel the new generation, the young generation, should learn and know what was going on in Germany. Because, God forbid, we don't want anything like that to happen over there again, and over here too. Now, we went-- we went to the museum of the Holocaust in Washington. And we have talked to our daughter-in-law, and of course, our son and our granddaughter, please to go and see it. It is of great importance that the young generation does not close their eyes. Even they should go to see the movie, which is unbelievable. I remember when we came out of and Fred said to me, let's go and have a bite to eat. And I couldn't. I was in tears, to see what has happened to the Jews over there, it's unbearable to. No, I feel your very strongly that the young generation should learn about it, have open eyes, that God forbid, this will never, never happen again. By the way, Fred and I went seven years ago to Berlin. I had an invitation to Berlin. And I went with very mixed feelings. And we did meet people who said, well, you know, we never meant that-- what it was. We never realized what was going on behind the scenes. But they must have closed their eyes for that. You saw what was going on, and the horrible things could never be wiped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I can only say, here, to the young people, open your eyes. Go to the museum. See what has happened, and ask your parents who have gone through it. Mrs. Bergmann, I want to thank you on behalf of the Shoah Visual Foundation for sharing your experiences with us, and opening your heart to us. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you very much. I think you were of great help to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please introduce the gentleman next to you, Mrs. Bergmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, this is Fred, and we are married-- this November 28, we will be married 44 years. And we are both-- have had very happy times together, and thank God we are both well. And we are both so happy that we have friends in our synagogue. We go together to the synagogue. And we go to concerts together, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the opera.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and the opera, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We have a subscription to the Metropolitan and to the City Opera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- otherwise, since we live so far from Manhattan, we wouldn't go. We don't have a car, so you don't go and just buy tickets. But this way, we know we are going on such and such a day, and make an effort. And it's wonderful. And Eva has been the most wonderful wife. And I'm happy, even though it's only 44 years in December, not in November. But that doesn't matter. Was the best thing I ever did, to marry her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to me too. He-- he was the best thing I have ever--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, once again--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was marvelous when I had the heart attack, and supportive. She visited me for seven weeks, every single day in the hospital from Queens to Manhattan and back. And at night, she had to field the telephone calls of people who wanted to find out. It was a very bad time for her, but she went through it wonderfully. And she gave me such a support and help that it really was worthwhile to get well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's wonderful to hear, and let's hope that you shall celebrate many, many more anniversaries together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once again, I want to thank you for sharing your feelings and your thoughts with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, thank you very much. This is a picture of my father, Professor Fritz Reiche. It was taken in the apartment in the Bronx where we lived, in 1954. He was still working at that time, and worked at the university in the Bronx, not anymore downtown. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He retired only when he was 75 years old. Yeah. Yeah. This is a picture of my mother, Bertha Reiche. And it was taken also in the same apartment in the Bronx. And she is holding something in her hand that we used in Germany. It was something to darn stockings, which you don't do anymore today. It was a-- a sort of a wooden-- wooden thing, with which you darned stockings. And she has that in her hand. She was-- 1954, Mum was-- she was born 1890, so she was 64 or 65, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a picture of the mother of my mother, my grandmother, Charlotte Ochs. In 1925, she gave it to me. There is a little writing underneath that picture. She gave it to me as a-- as a memory of the time when I lived with her as a child. I lived with her for, I think, two weeks once, and we had a wonderful time together. My grandmother lived with us later on before-- while we prepared our immigration. She lived with us in Berlin in Heilbronner Strasse. But unfortunately, we couldn't get her out anymore of Germany, and she was killed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she an educated person? What did she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she was actually not working. But she was the wife of a very famous conductor of the Philharmoniker, in Berlin, and was in a circle of many musical friends, of course. And she had a very rich life. And I do remember having spent holidays with her, even Christmas holidays, with a big Christmas tree, and we all had a wonderful time with her and her husband. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my family. We were in the mountains on a holiday. In the front is a picture of me. I was-- it was taken in 1923, so I was-- oh, it was taken 1922, I'm sorry. I was six years old. Then my grandmother sits beside me. She is the mother of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Laura Reiche. And she was a writer of books. On the left side is my brother Hans, who was two years older. So he was, on that picture, eight years. And then comes my mother, Bertha Reiche, and my father, Fritz. We were all in a mountain place and on vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were carrying in your lap, your doll?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- yes. I played a lot with dolls, and I have my little doll [INAUDIBLE] on my lap.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your brother often dress like that, or is--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was in a-- a dress that was in former years, children wore little outfits like that. They wear leather pants, and the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. A leather pants and-- Suspenders?

speaker: interviewee
text: --suspenders, right. That's what the children wore in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother in the middle. And on the left side is my brother. And on the right side, I am. And we are in Breslau in 1932, before Hitler came. Hans, my brother, went to school at that time still, and so was I in the lyceum. And we did a lot go out to the-- in Breslau was a big park, was called [GERMAN]. And we took quite a lot of excursions there. I know my mother was a very sporty type, and walked a lot. And Hans never liked too much to walk, but we all were sitting out there, relaxing in the sunshine. And I think the picture was taken by my father, in '32. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my very good and best friend, Bridget. She lives now in England. This picture was taken much later. It was taken in '47. And she sent it to me from England. She is the one who had-- with whom I had the terrible experience that in school, in high school, she was assaulted and verbally, terribly-- people talked terrible about her, and where the girl spit bread into her face. It was a terrible thing. But Bridget is now in England, and is quite a good friend, still, of mine. These two pictures, the one on the left side is a picture where I was in the children's and nursing home in Wannsee, where I had a year training. And I'm holding here a baby in my arms. This was the place where-- that was owned by a Jew, where he was taken out by Gestapos. And where I was able still to do my one year exam, state exam, as a babies' and children's nurse. The right one is a picture of me later on, in '38, March '38. It was before the Kristallnacht. And I was not working at that time, but it was taken on the street in front of our house, and I was-- look depressed. Somehow, I was not very happy at that time. And I look very depressed at that time, and-- but still well enough. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a book that was published 1993 by Thomas Powers. And it tells quite a lot about my father and his time in Berlin. And mainly about the message that he gave to American friends that was a secret message, that he gave to American friends in Princeton. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture that was taken about two months before we left Germany. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was not well at all at that time, as one can see. I was terribly undernourished, but we were in a garden. We were invited into a garden of our friends, and had a big dinner in their house. And this shows also, which is written underneath the picture, that Daddy committed-- brought a message to American friends about his-- the project of the bomb. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of Einstein in the University of Princeton. He works-- worked there with many other professors. Next to him sits Professor Ladenburg, to whom my father gave the secret message when he came over to America. And down below, Einstein is signing a letter for some student who had asked him to sign it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Professor-- Professor Einstein was the one who sent an urgent message to the American consulate in Berlin, asking them to hurry the visa for us. And he was also the one who wrote a letter of recommendation, so that they would let Daddy and Mom and me out earlier. He was a great help to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was taken-- this picture was taken on our wedding day, 28 of December 1951. One can see Fred on the side, on the left side, with me standing. I wore a very pretty light blue dress on that day. Then mother-- Mum. Then comes my sister-in-law, Sylvia, who was Hans' wife, still Hans' wife, and my father. It was taken at Loring Place in the Bronx, where my parents and I lived while Dad taught at the university in the Bronx, in the-- in-- so we lived in that rather primitive apartment. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in the Catskills, on vacation of my parents. And Peter, Fred, and I used to go and visit them. Now, on the left side, you see my mother. Then I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was Bertha Reiche. Then, besides her, it's me, then comes Peter-- who was, at that time, '60-- he was-- yeah, seven years old. And then my father. We always had a marvelous time in the Catskills with my parents. We went on walks together, and the house where they stayed was a house which was also by immigrants, and Dad enjoyed talking to the owner the house. And they had a lot of fun with them. And at night there was entertainment, musical entertainment. They really had a good time on their vacation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is a picture we had forgotten to take out, a picture of my grandmother, Charlotte Ochs, and her husband, Siegfried Ochs, who was a very famous conductor of the Philharmoniker in Berlin. They spent their vacation in Switzerland, and this is a picture of both of them in '27. My grandfather died 1929. My grandmother was with us.
